front: What is the relationship between AI, ML, and DL?
back: Nested subsets: AI (machines doing intelligent tasks) ⊃ ML (learning patterns from data instead of explicit programming) ⊃ DL (ML using multi-layer neural networks).
Supervised: learns from labeled data (classification, regression). Unsupervised: finds structure in unlabeled data (clustering, dimensionality reduction).
An agent learns by interacting with an environment and maximizing cumulative reward through trial and error (e.g. robotics, game playing, RLHF).

front: What happens during one training iteration?
back: Forward pass produces predictions → loss function measures error → backpropagation computes gradients → optimizer (e.g. Adam/SGD) updates weights.
Epoch: one full pass over the training dataset. Batch: subset of samples processed together. Iteration: one weight update (one batch).

front: Training vs inference: key performance metric
back: Training: throughput (samples/tokens per second, time-to-train). Inference: latency per request, balanced against throughput/cost (often via batching).

front: Why does training need more memory than inference?
back: Training holds weights + activations + gradients + optimizer states (several × model size). Inference holds only weights + working activations (+ KV cache for LLMs).
In LLM inference, stored attention keys/values for already-generated tokens so they aren't recomputed each step; it grows with context length and dominates inference memory.

front: Why do GPUs beat CPUs for deep learning?
back: DL is dominated by massively parallel matrix math; GPUs offer thousands of cores, huge memory bandwidth (HBM), and Tensor Cores. CPUs optimize few-core serial/branchy work.
CUDA core: general scalar/vector arithmetic unit. Tensor Core: specialized unit doing whole matrix multiply-accumulate ops per clock in reduced/mixed precision (TF32/FP16/BF16/FP8/FP4).

front: When is a CPU the right choice in an AI pipeline?
back: Serial/branchy work: data preprocessing and ETL logic, orchestration/control plane, small classical ML models, general business logic.
NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and programming model plus toolkit (compiler, runtime, libraries) for general-purpose GPU computing.
GPU-accelerated library of deep learning primitives (convolution, attention, normalization) used under the hood by PyTorch and TensorFlow.
NVIDIA's GPU-accelerated BLAS library for dense linear algebra (matrix multiply etc.); frameworks call it for GEMM operations.

NVIDIA Collective Communications Library: optimized multi-GPU/multi-node collectives (all-reduce, all-gather, broadcast) used for gradient sync in distributed training; topology-aware (NVLink, InfiniBand).
NVIDIA Data Loading Library: GPU-accelerated data loading and preprocessing (decode/augment) pipelines that keep GPUs fed during training.
NVIDIA's GPU-accelerated data science suite: cuDF (pandas-like dataframes), cuML (scikit-learn-like ML), running on GPUs.
Inference optimizer and runtime: layer/kernel fusion, precision calibration (FP16/FP8/INT8), kernel auto-tuning — produces a fast engine for a target NVIDIA GPU.
TensorRT-based open-source library specialized for LLM inference: kernel optimizations, quantization, in-flight batching, multi-GPU tensor parallelism.
Open-source model serving platform: hosts models from multiple frameworks behind HTTP/gRPC with dynamic batching, concurrent execution, ensembles, and Prometheus metrics.
NVIDIA Inference Microservices: prebuilt containers packaging a model + optimized runtime (e.g. TensorRT-LLM) + standard API (OpenAI-compatible), deployable anywhere; part of NVIDIA AI Enterprise.
NVIDIA GPU Cloud catalog: GPU-optimized containers, pretrained models, Helm charts, and SDKs — tested and optimized for NVIDIA platforms.

front: What does NVIDIA AI Enterprise provide over free downloads?
back: Enterprise support with SLAs, security patches and stable long-term branches, certification on mainstream servers/VMware/clouds, and production components like NIM.

front: Stages of the AI development lifecycle
back: Problem definition → data collection/prep → model training → evaluation/validation → deployment optimization (quantization/TensorRT) → deployment/serving → monitoring & retraining. A loop.
Degradation of model accuracy over time as real-world data distribution shifts from training data; detected by monitoring; fixed by retraining on fresh data.
Fine-tuning: further train a model on domain data. Prompting: steer via input text only. RAG: retrieve relevant documents at query time and feed them as context — no weight changes.
A large model pretrained on broad data that can be adapted (prompted/fine-tuned) to many downstream tasks instead of training per-task from scratch.
The dominant neural architecture for LLMs: self-attention layers let every token weigh every other token, enabling parallel training on sequences (unlike RNNs).

front: Common AI use cases by industry (name 4)
back: Healthcare: medical imaging diagnostics. Finance: fraud detection. Retail: recommender systems. Automotive: autonomous driving. (Also: manufacturing visual inspection, conversational AI.)

front: Why does batching increase inference throughput?
back: Processing many requests in one kernel launch amortizes overhead and fills the GPU's parallel units; trade-off: larger batches raise per-request latency.
A100 (2020): first MIG support (up to 7 instances), TF32 Tensor Cores, structured sparsity, 40/80 GB HBM2e, 3rd-gen NVLink.
H100 (2022): 4th-gen Tensor Cores with FP8, Transformer Engine, NVLink 4 (900 GB/s), HBM3; H200 variant has 141 GB HBM3e.
B200/GB200 (2024): dual-die GPU, FP4/FP6 inference formats, NVLink 5 (1.8 TB/s per GPU), GB200 NVL72 rack-scale NVLink domain (72 GPUs), liquid-cooled designs.
NVIDIA's Arm-based datacenter CPU, designed for high memory bandwidth and to pair coherently with NVIDIA GPUs (Grace Hopper GH200, Grace Blackwell GB200 superchips).
Coherent chip-to-chip interconnect (~900 GB/s) linking Grace CPU to Hopper/Blackwell GPUs in superchips, letting the GPU access CPU memory far faster than PCIe.
DGX: NVIDIA's turnkey integrated AI system (hardware + DGX software stack + NVIDIA support). HGX: the 8-GPU SXM baseboard with NVSwitch that OEMs build their own servers around.
8× H100 SXM GPUs, 4× NVSwitch, dual x86 CPUs, ConnectX-7 400G NICs (one per GPU) + BlueField DPUs, NVMe storage, DGX OS/Base Command software; ~10.2 kW max.
NVIDIA's modular server reference architecture letting OEMs build many configurations (including Grace CPUs and various GPU counts) with shared building blocks.
Validated reference architectures for DGX clusters: BasePOD = smaller scale; SuperPOD = large scale (tens to hundreds of nodes) with InfiniBand fabric, parallel storage, and Base Command management.

front: SXM vs PCIe GPU form factor
back: SXM: socketed module on HGX baseboard, full NVLink/NVSwitch to all peers, higher power (H100: 700 W). PCIe: standard slot card, lower power, at most NVLink bridge pairs — inter-GPU traffic mostly over PCIe.
NVIDIA's high-speed point-to-point GPU interconnect; H100 (NVLink 4): 900 GB/s total per GPU; Blackwell (NVLink 5): 1.8 TB/s — an order of magnitude above PCIe.
A switch chip building an all-to-all NVLink fabric so every GPU in a system (or NVL72 rack) talks to every other at full NVLink bandwidth simultaneously.
~64 GB/s per direction (~128 GB/s bidirectional) — compare NVLink 4's 900 GB/s per H100.

front: Why does data-parallel training stress the interconnect?
back: Every step ends with an all-reduce synchronizing gradients (≈ model size) across all GPUs; slow links leave GPUs idle and destroy scaling efficiency.
Data: replicate model, split data, sync gradients. Tensor: split each layer's matrices across GPUs (needs NVLink-class bandwidth). Pipeline: split model into layer stages, micro-batches flow through.
High Bandwidth Memory: DRAM stacked on the GPU package with very wide interfaces; H100 ~3.35 TB/s, H200 (HBM3e) ~4.8 TB/s — versus tens of GB/s for CPU DDR.

front: GPU memory rule of thumb for model weights
back: ~2 bytes per parameter at FP16/BF16 (1 byte at FP8/INT8). A 70B model ≈ 140 GB FP16 → needs multiple GPUs or quantization; training needs several × more.
Ampere+ Tensor Core mode: FP32's 8-bit exponent (same range) with 10-bit mantissa — accelerates existing FP32 code on Tensor Cores automatically, usually without accuracy loss.
Both 16-bit. BF16 keeps FP32's 8-bit exponent (same dynamic range, no loss scaling needed) — preferred for training. FP16 has more mantissa but a narrow 5-bit exponent, needing loss scaling.

front: What is FP8 and which architecture introduced Tensor Core support?
back: 8-bit floating point (E4M3/E5M2 variants) halving memory and doubling math rate vs FP16; introduced with Hopper, managed by the Transformer Engine.
Hopper+ hardware/software feature that dynamically selects FP8/FP16 precision and scaling per layer for transformer models — FP8 speed with maintained accuracy.

front: Which precisions for training vs inference vs HPC?
back: Training: BF16/TF32 mixed precision (FP8 emerging). Inference: FP16/FP8/INT8/INT4 quantized (FP4 on Blackwell). HPC/scientific: FP64.
Converting model weights/activations to lower precision (INT8, FP8, INT4) for inference — less memory, higher throughput — with calibration or QAT to preserve accuracy.
Ampere+ Tensor Core feature: if at least 2 of every 4 weights are zero (after pruning), hardware skips zeros for up to 2× math throughput.
Volta (V100, 2017). Ampere added TF32 + sparsity; Hopper added FP8 + Transformer Engine; Blackwell added FP4.

front: Why is AI training traffic hard for networks?
back: Synchronized bursts (all-reduce) of elephant flows across all nodes at once; the step completes at the speed of the slowest flow, so tail latency and any packet loss stall every GPU.

Lossless by design (credit-based flow control), native RDMA, lowest latency, adaptive routing, in-network computing (SHARP), managed fabric (subnet manager/UFM). NVIDIA Quantum switches + ConnectX adapters.
Scalable Hierarchical Aggregation and Reduction Protocol: InfiniBand switches perform reductions (e.g. gradient sums) in-network, reducing all-reduce data movement and latency.
RDMA over Converged Ethernet: InfiniBand-style RDMA verbs on Ethernet. Needs a lossless-tuned fabric (PFC, ECN) to perform; attractive where Ethernet operations are standard.
NVIDIA's AI-optimized Ethernet platform: Spectrum-4 switches + BlueField-3 SuperNICs with adaptive routing and congestion control, closing most of the gap to InfiniBand while staying Ethernet.

front: InfiniBand vs Ethernet/RoCE: how to choose
back: Max performance/scale for a dedicated training fabric → InfiniBand (SuperPOD default). Ethernet-standardized operations, multi-vendor policy, or cloud alignment → RoCE/Spectrum-X.
The RDMA NIC reads/writes GPU memory directly over PCIe, eliminating the copy through CPU system memory — lower latency and CPU load for multi-node training (used by NCCL).
Direct DMA between storage (NVMe/remote) and GPU memory, bypassing the CPU bounce buffer — faster data loading for training and analytics.
SuperPOD network design where each GPU's dedicated NIC connects to its own leaf-switch 'rail' (8 rails for 8 GPUs/node) matching NCCL traffic patterns and minimizing congestion.

Data Processing Unit: programmable SoC (Arm cores + NIC + accelerators) offloading infrastructure services — networking, storage, security — from host CPUs and isolating them from tenants. NVIDIA's is BlueField.
Networking (virtual switching, overlays), storage (NVMe-oF, virtualization), security (firewall, encryption, zero-trust microsegmentation) — freeing host CPU cores for applications.
NVIDIA's SDK/framework for programming BlueField DPUs — the DPU equivalent of what CUDA is to GPUs.

front: Power draw: DGX H100 node and AI racks
back: DGX H100 ≈ 10.2 kW max per node; AI racks run ~40–120+ kW (GB200 NVL72 ≈ 120 kW) vs ~10–15 kW traditional racks — power and cooling become first-order design constraints.
When rack density exceeds air-cooling limits (~40 kW/rack practical); Blackwell NVL72 rack-scale systems are liquid-cooled by design. Benefits: much higher density, better energy efficiency (PUE).
Power Usage Effectiveness = total facility power ÷ IT equipment power. Closer to 1.0 is better (1.1 excellent; 2.0 means half the power goes to overhead like cooling).

front: Storage tiers in an AI cluster
back: Local NVMe (per-node scratch/cache) → shared parallel/high-performance file system (Lustre, Spectrum Scale, VAST, WEKA — hot data, checkpoints) → object storage (datasets, archives).

front: Why does storage throughput affect GPU utilization?
back: If storage can't feed the data loaders, GPUs idle waiting for batches (data starvation); checkpoint write bursts can also stall training. Expensive accelerators should never wait on I/O.

front: Cloud vs on-prem for AI: decision drivers
back: Cloud: OPEX, instant start, elasticity — wins for bursty/short projects. On-prem: CAPEX, better TCO at sustained high utilization, data sovereignty/gravity, custom fabrics. Hybrid: steady base on-prem + cloud burst.
NVIDIA's AI-supercomputing-as-a-service: SuperPOD-class DGX infrastructure hosted in partner clouds and rented with NVIDIA software and expertise included.
Large datasets are costly/slow to move (plus egress fees) so compute gets placed where data lives — a primary force in on-prem vs cloud decisions.

front: How does a Kubernetes pod get a GPU?
back: NVIDIA device plugin advertises `nvidia.com/gpu` to the kubelet; the pod requests it in resources.limits; scheduler places it on a node with a free GPU; container toolkit injects driver libraries.

front: What does the NVIDIA GPU Operator do?
back: Automates the GPU software stack on K8s nodes: containerized driver, container toolkit, device plugin, DCGM exporter, GPU feature discovery/labels, MIG manager — no manual per-node installs.
The dominant HPC batch workload manager: job queues, partitions, priorities/fair-share, gang scheduling of multi-node jobs; GPUs scheduled via GRES (`--gres=gpu:4`); sbatch submits, srun launches.

Slurm: batch training clusters, queued multi-node HPC-style jobs, fair-share between research teams. Kubernetes: containerized services and inference, autoscaling, cloud-native ML platforms. Many orgs run both.
NVIDIA's cluster management software for provisioning/imaging nodes, managing drivers and software lifecycle, monitoring health, and deploying Slurm or Kubernetes on DGX PODs.

nvidia-smi: per-node CLI for spot checks (utilization, memory, temp, power, processes, ECC counts). DCGM: datacenter-grade fleet monitoring — background health checks, active diagnostics (dcgmi diag), policies, job stats, Prometheus exporter.
DCGM exporter (via GPU Operator) exposes Prometheus metrics → Prometheus scrapes → Grafana dashboards + alerts; metrics labeled with pod/namespace for per-workload attribution.

front: Key GPU health metrics to watch
back: Utilization, memory used, temperature, power draw, ECC errors (single/double-bit), XID events, NVLink errors, clock throttling reasons (thermal/power).
Single-bit: detected and corrected, job continues — watch the rate. Double-bit: uncorrectable — app typically crashes, pages retired; recurring errors → drain node / RMA GPU.
A GPU error event code logged by the NVIDIA driver (e.g. Xid 48 = double-bit ECC; Xid 79 = GPU fell off the bus) — first thing to check when a GPU job dies unexpectedly.

Multi-Instance GPU (Ampere+): hardware-partitions one GPU into up to 7 instances with dedicated SM/memory/cache slices — full memory/fault isolation and QoS; each instance appears as a GPU (e.g. `nvidia.com/mig-1g.10gb` in K8s).

MIG: hardware isolation, guaranteed memory/QoS — right for multi-tenant and latency-sensitive inference. Time-slicing: software context-switching, no memory isolation or QoS — fine for dev/bursty sharing and oversubscription.
When GPUs must be shared across virtual machines: VDI and VMware/enterprise-virtualization environments. Licensed NVIDIA driver/manager stack; can use MIG-backed or time-sliced profiles inside VMs.

front: Why run DCGM diagnostics in a job prologue?
back: A short active health check before each job keeps failing GPUs (memory/NVLink/thermal issues) out of large multi-node jobs — much cheaper than a big training run crashing mid-flight.
GPU orchestration/scheduling platform on Kubernetes (acquired by NVIDIA): fractional GPUs, quotas and fair-share pooling between teams, workload-aware batch scheduling for AI clusters.
